coredata.dc:title: Dynamic chloride ion repulsion facilitated by graphene quantum dots for stable electrolytic seawater oxidation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yuyang
Wang, Peng
Shi, Qiaofu
Hu, Fucheng
Zhang, Xinwei
Xu, Xue
Zhang, Jun
Zheng, Jie
Long, Yunze
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogen production via seawater electrolysis, leveraging sustainable energy sources such as offshore wind or solar energy, has immense application potential. However, the abundance of chloride ions (Cl−) in seawater leads to the generation of chlorine gas and hypochlorite at the anode during electrolysis, posing a severe threat of corrosion of the catalyst and electrolytic equipment. Herein, we synthesize a NiMo-based catalyst adorned with surface-anchored graphene quantum dots (GQDs). This catalyst possesses excellent Cl− exclusion capabilities. The Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs core–shell nanorod catalyst requires only 170 mV of overpotential to attain a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and operates stably for 200 h without degradation across a broad current density range from 100 to 400 mA cm−2. This remarkable electrocatalytic stability arises from the dynamic and efficient repulsion of Cl− at the catalytic interface, as proven by the post-reaction analysis of Cl− distribution within the catalyst. Furthermore, a potentiodynamic polarization test revealed that the Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs catalyst has high corrosion potential (0.66 V) and low corrosion current density (122.93 μA cm−2), underscoring its excellent corrosion resistance. This research presents a novel approach to mitigate Cl− corrosion during hydrogen production through seawater electrolysis, laying a solid foundation for advancing sustainable energy conversion technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrolytic seawater
OER
Stability
GQDs
Inhibit chlorine chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of the electrocatalytic OER performance

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22103045 and 52273077), and the State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles, Qingdao University (Nos. ZDKT202108, RZ2000003334, and G2RC202022).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural analysis of the Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs (GQDs: graphite quantum dots) grown on NF. (a) Schematic diagram of the catalyst synthesis. (b, c) SEM images of the catalyst at different magnifications. (d) TEM image and (e) STEM-EDS elemental mapping of the catalyst. (f) HRTEM image of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs core–shell nanorods. (g) HRTEM image of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs nanorods, with inset showing the corresponding SAED pattern. (h) HAADF-STEM image of the Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs.

Characterizations of the Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs catalysts. (a) Coordination structure model of the Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs core–shell nanorods. (b) XRD patterns of the Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs and Mo–NiS/Se catalysts. XPS patterns of (c) Ni 2p, (d) Mo 3d, (e) S 2p, and (f) Se 3d of the Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs and Mo–NiS/Se catalysts.

Electrocatalytic performance of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs. (a) OER polarization curves. (b) Bar chart with error bars comparing the performances of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs, Mo–NiS/Se, and RuO2 at current densities of 10, 100, and 300 mA cm−2, respectively. (c) Comparison of Tafel slopes of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs, Mo–NiS/Se, RuO2, and NF. (d) EIS of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs, Mo–NiS/Se, RuO2, and NF. (e) Linear fitting of capacitive current density and scan rate. (f) Performance comparison with various reported OER catalysts at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 in alkaline seawater. (g) Comparison of the stability of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs and Mo–NiS/Se at a current density of 100 mA cm−2 and the long-term stability of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs at a current density of 400 mA cm−2. (h) Polarization curves of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs before and after 20,000 CV cycles.

Corrosion resistance of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs against Cl− and its mechanisms. (a) Schematic diagram of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs operating in alkaline seawater. (b) TOF-SIMS depth distribution of C, Cl−, and OH− species in Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs and Mo–NiS/Se after 2000 CV cycles. (c) OCP measurement. (d) Electrochemical polarization curves of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs and Mo–NiS/Se in alkaline seawater.

head.ce:title: Dynamic chloride ion repulsion facilitated by graphene quantum dots for stable electrolytic seawater oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Key Laboratory of Medical and Health Textile Materials, College of Physics, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Key Laboratory of Medical and Health Textile Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aShandong Key Laboratory of Medical and Health Textile Materials, College of Physics, Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Industrial Research Institute of Nonwovens & Technical Textiles, Shandong Center for Engineered Nonwovens (SCEN), College of Textiles Clothing, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Industrial Research Institute of Nonwovens & Technical Textiles

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bIndustrial Research Institute of Nonwovens & Technical Textiles, Shandong Center for Engineered Nonwovens (SCEN), College of Textiles Clothing, Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266071, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogen production via seawater electrolysis, leveraging sustainable energy sources such as offshore wind or solar energy, has immense application potential. However, the abundance of chloride ions (Cl−) in seawater leads to the generation of chlorine gas and hypochlorite at the anode during electrolysis, posing a severe threat of corrosion of the catalyst and electrolytic equipment. Herein, we synthesize a NiMo-based catalyst adorned with surface-anchored graphene quantum dots (GQDs). This catalyst possesses excellent Cl− exclusion capabilities. The Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs core–shell nanorod catalyst requires only 170 mV of overpotential to attain a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and operates stably for 200 h without degradation across a broad current density range from 100 to 400 mA cm−2. This remarkable electrocatalytic stability arises from the dynamic and efficient repulsion of Cl− at the catalytic interface, as proven by the post-reaction analysis of Cl− distribution within the catalyst. Furthermore, a potentiodynamic polarization test revealed that the Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs catalyst has high corrosion potential (0.66 V) and low corrosion current density (122.93 μA cm−2), underscoring its excellent corrosion resistance. This research presents a novel approach to mitigate Cl− corrosion during hydrogen production through seawater electrolysis, laying a solid foundation for advancing sustainable energy conversion technologies.

ce:section.ce:para: Under the impetus provided by global energy transformation and environmental protection, a modern, sustainable new energy system needs to be urgently explored and developed [1,2]. Green hydrogen, produced through electrolytic water technology powered by renewable energy sources such as sun and wind, represents a zero-carbon emission and high-energy-density renewable energy carrier [2–4]. However, the conventional water electrolysis technology requires ultrapure deionized water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm. This requirement escalates the cost of hydrogen production and exacerbates the freshwater resource crisis [5,6]. Given the abundance of seawater resources and the potential for harnessing offshore wind, solar, and other renewable energy sources, hydrogen production through seawater electrolysis has emerged as an innovative and promising strategy [7,8]. Nonetheless, this strategy has certain challenges [9–11]. The high concentration of Cl− in seawater favors a two-electron transfer chlorine oxidation reaction (ClOR) at the anode during electrolysis. The ClOR competes with the desired oxygen evolution reaction (OER), drastically compromising the Faraday efficiency of water electrolysis [1,12,13]. Moreover, the generation of by-products such as chlorine gas or hypochlorite accelerates catalyst corrosion, posing a formidable challenge to the long-term stable operation of the electrolyzer [14,15]. Therefore, the application of traditional catalysts in seawater electrolysis for hydrogen production is limited, necessitating the re-evaluation and the design of more efficient, stable, and corrosion-resistant OER catalysts [16–18]. Therefore, an ultrahigh-corrosion-resistance catalyst that can sustain efficient OER activity in the complex seawater electrolysis environment is crucial for commercializing hydrogen production from seawater electrolysis and ultimately facilitating the green transformation of the global energy structure [19]. Motivated by this urgent need, the present study explores and optimizes high-performance, corrosion-resistant OER catalysts. This study aims to provide a solid scientific foundation and technical support for realizing sustainable, economical, and environmentally friendly green hydrogen production.

NiMo-based catalysts are characterized by surface electronic states similar to the precious metal Ir and robust synergy between the electron-rich Ni and electron-poor Mo. Owing to these properties, NiMo catalysts have emerged as a forefront material for enhancing OER performance [20,21]. To fully harness the potential of these catalysts, researchers have relentlessly pursued advanced strategies, including interface engineering and electronic structure modulation, to augment their intrinsic activity [21–24]. Xiao et al. [25] devised an innovative approach, leveraging the tensile compression strain effect induced by dense edge dislocations to develop an edge dislocation strain-enhanced Ni3Mo integrated electrode. Their strategy optimizes the electronic configuration of the dual active sites of Ni and Mo, forming a catalyst that demonstrates remarkable long-term stability for 200 h at a high current density of 500 mA cm−2. Wang et al. [26] constructed a Ni–S–Mo nanosheet heterostructure by introducing sulfur into the system. This design fosters the efficient adsorption of hydrogen- and oxygen-containing intermediates and elevates the catalytic performance in the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and OER. When assembled into an alkaline electrolyzer, the resultant bifunctional catalyst achieves a current density of 10 mA cm−2 at a low voltage of merely 1.53 V and maintains stable operation for 12 h. Despite these significant advancements in enhancing OER performance, the practical application of NiMo-based catalysts, particularly in the harsh environment of electrolytic seawater for hydrogen production, remains challenging. Because of the volatile electronic properties of Ni and the tendency of Mo to undergo oxidation-dissolution in electrochemical environments, these catalysts are susceptible to corrosion by chlorination products [27,28]. This limitation constrains the applicability of NiMo-based catalysts across a broader spectrum of scenarios. Hence, there is an urgent need to explore innovative strategies to boost their corrosion resistance and stability. Such endeavors are pivotal to advancing the development of hydrogen production technologies leveraging electrolytic seawater [29,30].

Improving the corrosion resistance of electrocatalysts, particularly in the harsh environment of seawater electrolysis for hydrogen production is a major challenge [31,32]. An innovative and effective strategy is to design a protective or repulsive layer on the catalyst surface to hinder or minimize the adsorption of undesired Cl− ions at the catalytically active sites. This approach can prolong the lifespan of the catalyst while maintaining its high performance [33–35]. Ling et al. [36] pioneered this field by introducing hard Lewis acids onto the catalyst surface, creating a localized alkaline microenvironment that selectively attracts hydroxyl ions (OH−) while repelling acidic Cl−. This strategy has demonstrated remarkable stability even in untreated seawater. However, a notable drawback of the conventional barrier and repulsion layer technologies is their tendency to encapsulate the catalyst surface completely, complicating the preparation process and potentially shielding the active sites, thereby compromising catalytic efficiency [37]. To circumvent this limitation, corrosion-resistant catalysts can be developed using graphene quantum dots (GQDs), an emerging graphene-based material. GQDs, with abundant active sites and inherent negative charge characteristics, form an ideal platform for constructing a space charge repulsion layer that effectively prevents Cl− adsorption [38–40]. By strategically incorporating GQDs into catalyst design, the catalytic activity can be preserved or even enhanced, and the resistance to Cl− erosion can be remarkably enhanced. This advancement presents a new approach for commercializing hydrogen production via seawater electrolysis. Therefore, an in-depth exploration of the potential of GQDs in constructing corrosion-resistant catalyst surface layers will have major theoretical implications and application prospects, underscoring the urgency and importance of this research endeavor.

Herein, we report the fabrication of a NiMo-based catalyst with GQDs anchored on its surface via a straightforward hydrothermal approach. This innovative design preserves the remarkable performance of the NiMo catalyst in OER and endows the catalyst with exceptional corrosion resistance through the unique properties of GQDs. Therefore, the catalyst exhibits outstanding stability and activity in alkaline seawater electrolysis environments. Specifically, the catalyst requires low overpotentials of 170 and 395 mV to reach current densities of 10 and 300 mA cm−2, respectively, in alkaline seawater. Notably, it operates steadily for 200 h without degradation across a broad current density range of 100–400 mA cm−2, exhibiting exceptional durability and stability. An in-depth mechanistic investigation revealed that GQDs are pivotal in the catalytic process. They dynamically repel Cl− ions, effectively preventing their adsorption and accumulation on the catalyst surface, thereby mitigating Cl− erosion. This conclusion is strongly fortified by experimental evidence, as demonstrated by a significant reduction in Cl− concentration at the catalyst interface after the reaction. Furthermore, potentiodynamic polarization tests revealed a high corrosion potential (0.66 V) and a low corrosion current density (122.93 μA cm−2) for the Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs catalyst, demonstrating its superior corrosion resistance. This discovery demonstrates the immense potential of GQDs in enhancing the corrosion resistance of catalysts and offers valuable insights and benchmarks for designing more efficient and stable hydrogen production catalysts customized for seawater electrolysis in the future.

Selenium powder (Se, 99.9 %), thiourea (CH4N2S, 99 %), molybdenum trioxide (MoO3, 99.5 %), ammonium fluoride (NH4F, 98 %), potassium hydroxide (KOH, 90 %), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs, 1 mg mL−1) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethanol (EtOH, ≥99.8 %), hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O, 85 %), and sodium borohydride (NaBH, 98 %) were purchased from Shanghai Hushi Laboratory Equipment Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Deionized water (DI) was purchased from Shanghai MacLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used directly without further purification.

First, the NF was immersed in 1 M hydrochloric acid solution for 5 min to remove surface oxides. Subsequently, the acid-treated NF was thoroughly rinsed with deionized water, acetone, and ethanol in sequence to ensure the complete removal of residual impurities. The cleaned NF was then dried in a vacuum oven to prevent further oxidation.

First, 0.288 g of selenium powder was dissolved in 10 mL of hydrazine hydrate, stirring for 30 min. Then, 0.278 g thiourea and 0.6 g ammonium fluoride were dissolved in 10 mL ethanol and 10 mL deionized water, stirring continuously for another 30 min. Following this, 0.144 g of molybdenum oxide was introduced into the mixture and stirred for another 30 min. Next, 0.002 g of sodium borohydride was cautiously added to the reaction mixture, and the stirring was continued at a reduced rate for 30 min. After thorough mixing, the resulting solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave along with a piece of dried NF (1 cm × 2.5 cm) as the substrate. The autoclave was then sealed and subjected to a solvothermal reaction at 200 °C for 24 h. Upon completion of the reaction and subsequent cooling, 1 mL of GQDs solution (1 mg mL−1) and 19 mL of deionized water were mixed and added to the autoclave. The reaction was further continued at 150 °C for 3 h to allow for the deposition of GQDs. After the solvothermal treatment, the autoclave was quickly cooled with water, and the Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs coated NF was removed. The product was thoroughly washed with deionized water three times to remove any residual impurities and then freeze-dried to remove moisture. Finally, the dried samples were stored in a vacuum desiccator to prevent oxidation and ensure long-term stability.
The synthesis of Mo–NiS/Se was similar to Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs except that the GOD solution was not added.

The morphology, nanostructure, and elemental composition of the samples were systematically investigated using a suite of advanced characterization techniques. Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM, Sigma500, operated at 30 kV) was employed for preliminary morphological analysis, complemented by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai F20, operated at 200 kV) and High-Angle Annular Dark-Field Scanning Transmission Electron Microscopy (HAADF-STEM, JEOL JEM-ARM200F) for detailed nanostructural insights. The composition of the nanocrystals was accurately determined using Inductively Coupled Plasma-Atomic Emission Spectrometry (ICP-AES). To elucidate the crystal structure of the samples, Powder X-ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using a Rigaku Smartlab diffractometer equipped with a 3 kW X-ray source. The chemical valence state and surface atomic ratios were meticulously assessed via X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) on a Thermo Fisher Scientific instrument, providing valuable information on the surface chemistry of the samples. Furthermore, the distribution depth of various elements on the surface post-electrochemical reaction was precisely measured using Time-of-Flight Secondary Ion Mass Spectrometry (TOF-SIMS, TOF-SIMS 5, iontof). This technique offers high sensitivity and depth resolution, enabling a comprehensive understanding of the surface composition and elemental redistribution during electrochemical processes.

All electrochemical measurements were performed at room temperature. The hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) electrocatalytic activities were evaluated in seawater containing 1 M KOH using a three-electrode configuration on a CS310X electrochemical workstation. For the overall water splitting, a two-electrode system was employed. The as-synthesized sample, serving as a 3D electrode, was utilized as the working electrode (1 cm × 1 cm), while a graphite rod and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) served as the counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. All potentials were calibrated to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) according to the Nernst equation. Prior to measuring cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), the working electrodes were activated through multiple CV cycles at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. LSV was conducted at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1, and the LSV measurements were corrected with 90 % iR compensation. Tafel plots were derived from the polarization curves by plotting the overpotential (η) against the logarithm of the current density (log |J|). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out over a frequency range of 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz under an AC voltage of 10 mV at 1.6 V vs. RHE for OER studies. To assess the durability of the catalysts, accelerated durability tests were conducted through continuous potential cycling between 1.1 and 2.0 V vs. RHE at a scan rate of 200 mV s−1 for 20,000 cycles. Chronopotentiometry tests were also performed for 300 h at a constant current density of 10 mA cm−2. The electrochemical double-layer capacitance (C dl) was measured using the CV method by sweeping the potential at various scan rates (20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 mV s−1) within a range where no faradic current was observed (0.49 to 0.59 V vs. RHE). The double-layer capacitance was calculated from the slopes of the plots of the half-difference of the positive and negative current densities at the midpoint of the scanning range (0.54 V) versus the scan rates. For the overall water splitting tests, a two-electrode system was utilized with the catalysts loaded onto nickel foam (NF) substrates at a mass loading of 2.4 mg cm−2. Polarization curves were obtained at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. When using commercial 20 wt.% RuO2 as a benchmark catalyst, 10 mg of RuO2 and 35 μL of 5 wt.% Nafion solution were dispersed in 965 μL of isopropanol through sonication to form a homogeneous ink. Subsequently, 240 μL of the prepared catalyst ink was drop-cast onto the surface of the NF, resulting in a catalyst loading of approximately 2.4 mg cm−2.

A Mo–NiS/Se core–shell nanorod catalyst with surface-anchored GQDs on NF was synthesized through a well-defined, stepwise hydrothermal method, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). In the initial hydrothermal step, H2S gas generated by the decomposition of thiourea at 200 °C reacted with NF, yielding NiS nanoparticles as seeds for further growth. Subsequently, selenium ions (Se2−) were introduced to facilitate the transition from NiS to NiS/Se nanorods through the partial substitution of sulfur atoms in NiS [41]. In this process, a portion of Ni dissolved in the solution, generating Ni2+ ions that promoted the continuity of the reaction and the formation of a complex structure. Subsequently, using potent reducing agents, i.e., hydrazine hydrate and NaBH4, amorphous Mo was doped onto the surface of NiS/Se nanorods, forming Mo–NiS/Se nanosheets [42–44]. In the second hydrothermal step at 150 °C, GQDs were uniformly anchored onto the surface of Mo–NiS/Se nanosheets, finally forming the core–shell nanostructure. This design leverages the negatively charged nature of GQDs to repel chloride ions effectively and drastically enhances the overall performance of the catalyst through the unique physicochemical properties of GQDs. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images (Fig. 1(b, c)) reveal that the core–shell nanorod arrays grow densely and uniformly on the NF surface, creating a three-dimensional structure that substantially increases the specific surface area of the catalyst and provides ample active sites for electrochemical reactions. The transmission electron microscopy image (TEM) (Fig. 1(d)) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (Fig. S1 in Supporting Information) further confirm that GQDs and nanosheets are uniformly anchored to the surface of the nanorods, validating the formation mechanism of the core–shell nanostructure.

To gain deeper insights into the elemental distribution and crystal structure of the synthesized samples, scanning TEM coupled with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (STEM-EDS) elemental mapping was performed. The results clearly show that Mo is successfully incorporated into the material, while Ni, S, and Se elements are uniformly distributed throughout the sample (Fig. 1(e)). This finding confirms the successful doping and homogeneity of the elements within the composite structure. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images reveal the intriguing microstructural differences within the material. In the nanosheet region, the absence of a long-range ordered atomic arrangement suggests the formation of an amorphous structure (Fig. 1(f)). Conversely, the nanorods exhibit distinct lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.27 nm, which matches the (101) crystal plane of NiS/Se (Fig. 1(g)). The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern further validates this lattice structure, revealing lattice spacings intermediate between those of NiS and NiSe; this finding is consistent with the substitution of S atoms by the larger Se atoms, resulting in lattice strain [45]. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern corroborates these findings, reinforcing the conclusion that Se doping into NiS leads to lattice expansion. The formation of the amorphous Mo–NiS/Se nanosheets is attributed to the role of ammonium fluoride (NH4F). In the initial stages of the reaction, abundant S2− and Se2− ions ensure unhindered nanorod growth [46]. However, as the reaction progresses, the depletion of these ions slows down nanorod elongation [47,48]. At later stages, NH4F forms macromolecules with S2− and Se2− (i.e., (NH4)2S and (NH4)2Se). These macromolecules act as capping agents that prolong the reaction, ultimately favoring the formation of amorphous nanosheets [49,50]. In addition, high-angle annular dark field transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-TEM) analysis reveals the presence of GQDs on the nanosheet surface. As shown in Fig. 1(h), randomly distributed white flakes with an average diameter of 5 nm exhibit a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (102) plane of GQDs, confirming that the GQDs are incorporated into the nanosheet structure. In summary, we synthesized Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs core–shell nanorod catalysts with NiS/Se nanorods as cores and Mo–NiS/Se amorphous nanosheets adorned with GQDs as shells. This unique architecture harnesses the complementary advantages of crystalline and amorphous materials while integrating the exceptional properties of GQDs, thereby promising exceptional performance and vast application potential in catalysis.

The amorphous-crystalline heterostructure of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs is illustrated in Fig. 2 (a). The NiS/Se nanorods have a long-range oblique crystal structure, whereas the Mo–NiS/Se nanorods display an isotropic, short-range ordered random arrangement within the atomic free volume region. To further examine the influence of GQDs on the structural characteristics, we prepared Mo–NiS/Se without GQDs for comparison. The XRD patterns of Mo–NiS/Se clarify that the incorporation of GQDs does not significantly alter the crystal structure of Mo–NiS/Se (Fig. 2(b)). Both samples remain highly consistent in material composition, establishing that GQDs primarily impact the electronic structure and surface properties of the material rather than its underlying crystal structure [39]. The chemical composition and element-binding state of the catalyst were thoroughly examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS spectrum of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs confirms the presence of Mo, Ni, S, and Se. Fig. 2(c, d) displays the Ni 2p and Mo 3d XPS spectra of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs and Mo–NiS/Se, respectively. In the Ni 2p XPS spectrum, peaks at binding energies of 855.7 and 873.4 eV correspond to Ni2+, those at 858 and 876.4 eV to Ni3+, and those at 852.3 and 869.5 eV to Ni0. Here, Ni2+ and Ni3+ are coordinated with S and Se, with Ni2+ occupying a dominant position, with a Ni2+:Ni3+ ratio of approximately 4:1. Ni0 originates from the residual unreacted NF [51,52]. Notably, compared to Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs, the Ni 2p core-level spectrum of Mo–NiS/Se does not exhibit any remarkable peak shifts, indicating that the coordination environment around Ni atoms remains largely unchanged in both structures. This finding suggests that GQDs have a minimal effect on Ni atomic coordination. The Mo 3d XPS spectrum shows an additional single peak at 226.3 eV, which is associated with the Mo–S ligand [45]. Peaks at 227.8 and 230.8 eV are assigned to the Mo element and potentially arise from the interaction of a strong reducing agent with MoO3 during the reaction [20,45]. The peaks at 229 and 233.4 eV correspond to Mo4+, and those at 232.2 and 235.3 eV to Mo6+. The Mo6+:Mo4+ ratio is approximately 6:1. Compared to Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs, the Mo6+ peak of Mo–NiS/Se exhibits a notable positive shift of ± 0.3 eV, accompanied by a weakened peak intensity, demonstrating that the introduction of GQDs modifies the local electronic structure and coordination environment around Mo atoms. This observation is consistent with the changes observed in the S 2p and Se 2p XPS spectra. Compared with Mo–NiS/Se, the S2− peak of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs shifts towards higher binding energies, whereas the Se2− peak shifts towards lower binding energies. This confirms that, under the influence of GQDs, Mo atoms exhibit a stronger binding ability with S atoms (Fig. 2(e, f)) [53,54]. Furthermore, the XPS spectra of Ni 2p and Mo 3d reveal that the Mo–Ni atomic ratio is 9.9:90.1 (Table S1). This finding confirms that Mo is exclusively doped into the amorphous NiS/Se nanosheets, and the introduction of GQDs primarily influences the electronic structure of Mo atoms, with minimal effect on Ni atoms [37,45,54,55]. In summary, the incorporation of GQDs effectively modulates the coordination environment of metal active sites and optimizes the electronic structure of Mo–NiS/Se core–shell nanorod catalysts, thereby exhibiting exceptional OER activity and stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the electrocatalytic OER performance
ce:section.ce:para: We conducted a series of rigorous electrochemical tests to assess the application potential of Mo–NiS/Se core–shell nanorod catalysts adorned with surface-anchored GQDs for hydrogen production via seawater electrolysis. The OER performance of the Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs catalyst was evaluated using a three-electrode system in an alkaline seawater environment. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves reveal that Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs require a low overpotential of only 170 mV, slightly outperforming the unmodified Mo–NiS/Se (172 mV) and significantly surpassing NF (717 mV) and the traditional precious metal catalyst RuO2 (322 mV) at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 (Fig. 3 (a)). Notably, at higher current densities of 100 and 300 mA cm−2, Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs also exhibited better performance, requiring overpotentials of 306 and 395 mV, respectively, compared to Mo–NiS/Se (325 and 415 mV, respectively) and RuO2 (488 and 698 mV, respectively), demonstrating its advantages (Fig. 3(b)). These findings underscore the significant OER activity enhancement imparted by GQDs incorporated into the Mo–NiS/Se catalyst. In addition, we also investigated the impact of the GQDs content on the OER performance of the catalyst. The material loaded with 0.5 mL of GQDs required 318 mV to reach a current density of 100 mA cm−2. This indicates that an increase in the GQDs content enhances the OER performance, albeit not significantly (Fig. S2). Further analysis of the reaction kinetics via the Tafel slope (Fig. 3(c)) reveals that the Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs catalyst has a Tafel slope of 62 mV dec−1, which is lower than that of Mo–NiS/Se (74 mV dec−1), RuO2 (157 mV dec−1), and NF (163 mV dec−1). This result indicates that the OER reaction kinetics of the Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs is faster. According to the electron transfer mechanism, the lower the Tafel slope in the rate-dependent step of the OER reaction, which indicates that the rate-dependent step at the end of the muti-electron transfer reaction, thus the electrocatalytic performance is better [56]. Additionally, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy analysis shows that Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs have the lowest charge transfer resistance (0.98 Ω) among the compared catalysts, highlighting the effective promotion of charge transfer on the catalyst surface by GQDs and thereby enhancing the overall electrocatalytic performance (Fig. 3(d)). The electrochemical double-layer capacitance (C dl) is calculated from the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curve (Fig. S3). The C dl value of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs is 26.67 mF cm−2, which is slightly higher than that of RuO2 (22.11 mF cm−2) and significantly higher than NF (5.18 mF cm−2) (Fig. 3(e)). This result suggests that the GQD modification exposes more active sites on the Mo–NiS/Se catalyst, favoring the OER reaction. This is also confirmed by the normalized electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) curve, which demonstrates that the catalytic performance of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs is significantly better than that of RuO2 and NF (Fig. S4). At higher current densities, the OER performance of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs is also superior, reaching a current density of 1 A cm−2 at 806 mV of overpotential, which outperforms Mo–NiS/Se, which requires 875 mV (Fig. S5) The Faraday efficiency of the Mo-NiS/Se@GQDs catalyst was determined by collecting reaction products using the drainage method after reacting for 2000 s at a current density of 90 mA cm−2. The calculation reveals that the Faraday efficiency of the catalyst is as high as 99.3 %, indicating minimal energy loss during the reaction (Fig. S6). This result demonstrates the excellent performance of the Mo-NiS/Se@GQDs catalyst in terms of energy conversion efficiency. To simulate the catalytic performance of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs in the actual application environment, we constructed a bifunctional membrane electrode electrolyzer for OER testing (Fig. S7). The results indicate that Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs require only 1.926 V to achieve a current density of 400 mA cm−2, which was superior to that of Mo–NiS/Se (2.04 V). This demonstrates that Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs also holds great potential for practical applications. A comparison of the OER activity of state-of-the-art catalysts in alkaline seawater (Fig. 3(f) and Table S2), establishes the excellent OER activity and notable advantages of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs over previously reported catalysts. Moreover, stability is crucial for the practical applications of seawater electrolysis for hydrogen production. The OER stabilities of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs and Mo–NiS/Se were evaluated at 100 mA cm−2 using chronopotentiometry (CP). As shown in Fig. 3(g), Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs exhibited no noticeable degradation after 100 h of continuous reaction, whereas the catalytic activity of Mo–NiS/Se significantly declined after approximately 4 h. The content of elements leached into the electrolyte after the stability test (Table S3) shows that the concentrations of Mo, Ni, and Se in the electrolyte of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs were 4.85, 0.22, and 4.71 mg L−1, respectively, which are much lower than the corresponding concentrations of 7.28, 2.11, and 8.52 mg L−1 in the electrolyte of Mo–NiS/Se. This demonstrates that GQDs have a positive effect on improving the corrosion resistance of the catalyst. We also investigated the effect of the GQDs content on stability. After 50 h of OER reaction at a current density of 100 mA cm−2 in alkaline seawater, the performance of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs with 1 mL of GQDs remained unchanged, while the performance of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs with 0.5 mL of GQDs decreased by 50 %. This indicates that a higher GQDs content leads to better OER stability for Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs (Fig. S8). Furthermore, we evaluated the OER stability of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs in alkaline seawater under industrial current density. As shown in Fig. 3(g), the OER performance at a current density of 400 mA cm−2 exhibits no discernible decline following 100 h of continuous operation, suggesting that Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs maintain exceptional stability even under high current density conditions. Moreover, the cycle stability of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs was rigorously tested. The results (Fig. 3(h)) indicate that the catalyst retained its initial catalytic activity even after 20,000 CV cycles. The morphology characterization of the catalyst post-stability testing (Fig. S9) reveals that the rod-like structure of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs is well preserved, without any alterations in the elemental composition compared to the pristine state. This finding demonstrates the high stability and corrosion resistance of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs in seawater hydrogen production. We have conducted additional post-reaction characterization of the Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs catalyst using HRTEM, XRD, XPS, and Raman spectroscopy to assess its stability after 50 h of stability testing during the OER. The HRTEM image (Fig. S10) reveals that the catalyst retains its primary structure, with lattice spacings of 0.27 nm corresponding to the (101) crystal face of NiS/Se on the nanorod and 0.22 nm matching the (102) crystal face of GQDs on the nanosheet. The amorphous structure on the nanosheets is still present, indicating minimal structural changes. In addition, we have compared the XRD patterns of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs before and after 50 h of stability testing (Fig. S11). The comparison reveals that despite the redox reactions, the main structure of the catalyst remained unchanged. The XPS spectra of Ni 2p, Mo 3d, S 2p, and Se 3d (Fig. S12) show no significant changes in the valence states of these elements after 50 h of stability testing, consistent with the XRD test results. This suggests that the catalyst maintains its composition and elemental states during the OER process. Finally, Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S13) was used to analyze the phase transition of the Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs before and after the stability test. The results indicate that the peak corresponding to NiS weakens, while the peak of NiOOH increases significantly after the reaction. This confirms that NiOOH is the main active site of the catalyst, which exhibits excellent catalytic activity. Notably, the Se–Se peak remains relatively unchanged, demonstrating the good stability of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs during the stability test. In summary, the post-reaction characterization confirms the excellent stability and corrosion resistance of the Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs catalyst during the OER process. This potential is attributed to its outstanding OER activity, efficient charge transfer capability, and remarkable stability.

The remarkable activity and stability of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs in alkaline seawater is primarily attributed to the pivotal role of GQDs. This discovery enriches our comprehension of the catalyst-electrolyte interface dynamics and offers a fresh perspective for advancing seawater hydrogen production technology. First, the negative charge properties of GQDs are particularly significant in alkaline seawater environments. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), these negative charges endow GQDs with a functionality akin to interlayer CO3 2−, effectively preventing the adsorption of Cl− ions from seawater and thereby inhibiting chlorination reaction on the catalyst surface [40,54]. Consequently, GQDs effectively protect the catalyst from chlorination-induced corrosion, resulting in excellent stability in alkaline seawater. Second, the XPS analysis reveals that integrating GQDs modifies the Mo6+ and Mo4+ contents. Notably, compared to Mo–NiS/Se, Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs have a higher proportion of Mo6+, and hence, a higher number of active catalytic sites. Furthermore, the electron-donating nature of GQDs potentially stabilizes the high-energy intermediate states of the surface active sites, enhancing the OER activity in alkaline seawater [38,57]. Third, the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the GQDs surface act as proton regulators during the OER reaction. These functional groups facilitate water dissociation and the proton-coupled electron transfer process, expediting the OER rate [38,39,54,58]. Notably, the Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs catalyst also exhibits higher HER activity than Mo–NiS/Se (Fig. S14), further substantiating the positive influence of GQDs on enhancing the overall electrocatalytic performance. In summary, GQDs significantly promote the stability and OER activity of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs catalyst in alkaline seawater through their negative charge characteristic, modulation of electronic structure, and role of proton regulators. Thus, a novel catalyst design concept is realized for seawater hydrogen production technology, and this finding offers valuable insights for other electrocatalytic reactions in complex electrolyte environments.

To further elucidate the specific interaction mechanism of the Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs catalyst with Cl− ions in alkaline seawater, we used a suite of advanced characterization techniques. First, depth profiling experiments were performed using time-of-flight secondary ion mass spectrometry (TOF-SIMS) to assess the penetration depth of Cl− ions within the catalysts. As shown in Fig. 4(b), after 2000 CV cycles, the Cl− ions distribution depth in Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs is notably shallower than that in Mo–NiS/Se. This direct observation highlights the significant repulsive effect of GQDs on Cl− ions, indicating that GQDs effectively shield the catalyst surface from Cl− ions during the OER reaction. Moreover, the C and OH− contents in Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs are substantially higher than those in Mo–NiS/Se, verifying the successful incorporation of GQDs (Table S4) [59]. To further assess the corrosion resistance of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs, we measured the open circuit potential (OCP) and potentiodynamic polarization curves in alkaline seawater. As shown in Fig. 4(c), the OCP of Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs (0.6948 V) exceeds that of Mo–NiS/Se (0.3882 V), indicating a lower tendency for corrosion in the former. The potentiodynamic polarization curve (Fig. 4(d)) reveals that Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs (0.66 V, 122.93 μA cm−2) have a higher corrosion potential and a lower corrosion current density than Mo–NiS/Se (0.609 V, 180.29 μA cm−2). Previous studies established that catalysts with higher corrosion potential and lower corrosion current densities have enhanced corrosion resistance [60]. Thus, incorporating GQDs significantly enhances the corrosion resistance of the catalyst. This enhanced corrosion resistance was further corroborated by the STEM-EDS elemental mapping of the Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs after 200 h of stability testing (Fig. S15). In summary, the Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs catalyst augments its OER activity in alkaline seawater and significantly improves its durability through the effective repulsion of Cl− ions and attraction of OH− ions mediated by GQDs. The enhanced corrosion resistance provides new avenues for the design and application of catalysts in seawater hydrogen production technologies.

In this study, we successfully synthesized Mo–NiS/Se core–shell heterogenic nanorod catalyst anchored onto GQD surfaces via a meticulously designed two-step hydrothermal method. The resultant catalyst shows remarkable activity and stability toward the OER under alkaline seawater conditions. Notably, it achieves high current densities of 100 and 300 mA cm−2 at low overpotentials of 306 and 395 mV, respectively. Furthermore, its performance remains unchanged even after continuous operation at an industrial-scale current density of 400 mA cm−2 for 100 h, underscoring its exceptional durability. The superior catalytic performance is primarily attributed to the Ni–Mo heterostructure, which modulates the local electronic configuration of metal atoms, thereby facilitating the OER kinetics by diminishing the binding energies of the hydrogen and oxygen intermediates. Additionally, the negative charge properties of GQDs result in the effective repulsion of Cl−, considerably contributing to the stability of the catalyst in seawater. The abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on GQDs further accelerate water dissociation and electron coupling transfer, enhancing the OER performance compared to Mo–NiS/Se without GQDs. In conclusion, we developed a high-performance OER catalyst customized for electrolytic seawater splitting and proposed a general strategy to mitigate chlorination and improve catalyst stability by incorporating GQDs. This novel approach opens up a new avenue for developing and deploying advanced electrode materials for seawater electrolysis. Further, this approach can promote the advancement of seawater-based hydrogen production technologies. We anticipate that with continued research and technological advancements, a wide range of applications of the Mo–NiS/Se@GQDs catalyst will be realized in the expanding field of clean energy.

Yuyang Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Peng Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Qiaofu Shi: Validation, Methodology, Data curation. Fucheng Hu: Validation, Data curation. Xinwei Zhang: Validation, Data curation. Xue Xu: Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jun Zhang: Resources, Conceptualization. Jie Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Methodology. Yunze Long: Writing – review & editing, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22103045 and 52273077), and the State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles, Qingdao University (Nos. ZDKT202108, RZ2000003334, and G2RC202022).
